data.title: Undercovered Dozen: Canadian Natural Resources, Armada Hoffler, Alexandria Real Estate And More
data.description:
The 'Undercovered' Dozen is a weekly Seeking Alpha editor-curated series highlighting 12 articles on lesser covered stocks from the previous seven days. We hope this provides ideas and inspires discussion among the community.

Today, we're looking at articles published between May 30–June 5.

Take a look at what these less-covered stocks might hold for you. And please join the conversation below to share what you think: Are any of these worth following up on? And are there other undercovered ideas that you like?

Canadian Natural Resources: My Number 1 Energy Pick

Jonathan Weber| Strong Buy

Canadian Natural Resources (NYSE:CNQ)(TSX:CNQ:CA) is an energy company that combines many positives: Strong growth, a nice dividend yield, resilience versus low oil prices and macro shocks, and so on. Add an undemanding valuation, and CNQ looks very compelling — it's not risk-free but looks like a good investment to me.

Armada Hoffler: The Knife Has Fallen, Time To Pick It Up

Roberts Berzins, CFA| Buy

My previous article on Armada Hoffler Properties, Inc. (AHH) was issued in February 2025 before the dividend cut was announced. The title of the article was: Armada Hoffler Properties: Catching A Falling Knife Or Diamond? I'm Still Long. As the title indicates, I was rather bullish on AHH. The main driver for my bullishness was the assumption of lower base rates and the associated tailwinds that should help AHH gradually emerge out of a very tight dividend coverage level. Plus, the underlying business seemed rather robust.

However, since the publication of my piece, the stock is down by ~20%.

The dividend cut in combination with additional equity raise and weaker-than-expected AFFO guidance gave the market a strong reason to assign even more aggressive risk premiums to AHH's valuation. Plus, the volatility in the long-term yields and the tariff-induced uncertainty have not helped AHH either.

Now, I think that by looking at the title of the current article, it should be clear what my stance is on AHH. I know that the ~20% decline after my previous thesis is not encouraging and does not necessarily provide confidence that this time the total return will be acceptable.

I fully get it.

Having said that, let me still articulate my thinking on why, in my view, AHH presents a very compelling risk-to-reward play.

Alexandria Real Estate: Undervalued, Yielding Over 7% With 14 Years Of Dividend Growth

Steven Fiorillo| Strong Buy

One of my buddies told me to look into Alexandria Real Estate (NYSE:ARE) last month. After going through the 10-K, several earnings calls, the most recent quarter, and tearing through the financials, I think there is massive value to be unlocked.

ARE reminds me of SL Green Realty Corp (SLG) when the regional banking crisis was occurring, and the narrative regarding landlords being forced to turn the keys over back to the banks for office buildings was forming. ARE is one of the largest REITs focused on the life sciences sector, as its properties meet the infrastructure and critical operational needs of collaborative life science facilities. ARE has 39.6 million rentable square feet of operating properties with an occupancy rate of 91.7%. While ARE had been stuck in an elongated downtrend since the Fed started increasing interest rates, shares have slid further after operating income, funds from operations (FFO), and EBITDA declined in the Q1 results despite ARE beating the consensus estimates on FFO and revenue. ARE saw a decrease in its net operating income (NOI) because of the dispositions throughout 2024, especially the $1.1 billion of dispositions completed during Q4 2024. Investors are able to get ARE at the lowest levels it's traded for in a decade, as shares are trading well below their pandemic lows.

What I like about ARE is that it's trading at a discount to book, a discount to equity yields over 7.5%, and could be a likely candidate for a re-rating when the Fed starts cutting rates.

Sila Realty Trust: This Is Why I Do My Own Due Diligence

Luuk Wierenga| Hold

I'm 100% invested in REITs. Because the dividends give me a nice additional cash flow that helps me fund my lifestyle and needs. I'm still young and working full-time, so the dividends are a nice additional bonus. Nothing more and nothing less. I don't feel it is necessary to decrease my exposure to REITs. Especially since we're still in a REIT bear market right now. So I'm still expanding my positions. More on this later on.

Even though I'm solely invested in REITs, diversification across different REITs is still important to me, in order to mitigate some concentration risks.

My followers know that I have a big position in Medical Properties Trust (MPW). Having written four in-depth articles in recent months. But I have trimmed this position lately. As of today, I am scanning the REIT market in order to capitalize on the great opportunities that I come across.

Since Sila Realty Trust (NYSE:SILA) is in the "Health Care" part of the REIT sector, I want to assess whether it would be a prudent step to swap some of my Medical Properties Trust shares for a new position in this REIT.

Today I will take you with me in my analysis of SILA, to see if it suits my REIT portfolio.

Athene Holding's ATHS Offers Attractive 8.2% IG Yield And Short Duration Exposure

Arbitrage Trader| Buy

In February 2025, we covered in a public article the Athene Holding Ltd. Rate Reset Junior Subordinated Debentures due 03/30/2064 (NYSE:ATHS), issued by Athene Holding Ltd., a subsidiary of Apollo Global Management, Inc. (NYSE:APO). Some time has passed, and ATHS is still among our holdings. In this short article, we'll look at the current metrics of the issue, put them in the context of issues with similar characteristics, and point out why it is still among our favourite investment positions.

B2Gold: Golden Goose About To Hatch

Macrotips Trading| Buy

It's been a while since I last wrote about B2Gold Corp. (NYSE:BTG). In my last article, I noted that mine delays and cost overruns at the company's Back River gold mine were causing BTG to underperform. Fast-forward 12 months, and B2Gold has certainly continued to lag behind its peers, with B2Gold's shares returning 24.8% in the past twelve months compared to 42.5% for the VanEck Gold Miners ETF (GDX) (Figure 1).

However, with Back River slated to achieve the first production imminently, I believe it is time to revisit the thesis on B2Gold.

With the addition of Back River, B2Gold's production profile should see a step-up to ~1.2 million oz run-rate. Furthermore, B2Gold has a slate of organic projects that can add another 400k oz in production over the next few years.

Despite the robust growth profile, B2Gold's valuation has been depressed, as investors fear development delays and cost overruns. B2Gold is trading at roughly a quarter of its peer group's EV/production valuation.

As long as Back River is completed in the next few months, I believe B2Gold's valuation should begin to re-rate higher. I rate BTG a buy.

Why I Just Bought Back SLB (Schlumberger)

Laura Starks | Buy

A few months ago, I sold SLB (SLB) (formerly known as Schlumberger) stock after the oil price decline, subsequent notices of drilling cutbacks, tariff effect uncertainty, and when a fellow Seeking Alpha member mentioned, in response to my last article for Seeking Alpha about SLB, that SLB’s price hadn’t moved much. Indeed, the company’s stock price is down 21% since my last article.

SLB expects to return at least $4 billion to shareholders in 2025 through dividends and share repurchases but has already bought back $2.3 billion, or most of the $2.5 billion allocated to share repurchases for the year. (Dividends are expected to total $1.5 billion.) And, its proposed ChampionX acquisition has been approved in the US but not yet in Norway.

SLB has not been immune to the recent drilling slowdown. With the (important) exception of cash flow from operations, its first-quarter 2025 financial results were below those of the year-ago quarter. ...

I am buying back the stock at its lower price. Although drilling activity is under pressure, there are regional distinctions. Many US companies are moving away from the Lower 48 and towards overseas. SLB is one of the few prominent in international oilfield services with the financial heft, technology, and experience to serve larger and overseas customers.

SLB’s financial metrics point to value, notably the 44% upside to its one-year target and a stock price at the low end of the 52-week range.

Circle Internet Group IPO: A Bet On Fiat Survival Through Stablecoins

Mike Fay| Hold

One of the 'crypto companies' that has long been expected to enter the public equity markets is set to begin trading on June 5th with the IPO of Circle Internet Group (NYSE:CRCL). Circle Internet is the issuer of the stablecoin USDC (USDC-USD). The company attempted to go public via the SPAC merger with Concord Acquisition Corp (CND) back in 2021, but the parties mutually agreed to terminate the planned combination after the US Securities and Exchange Commission did not declare the S-4 registration as effective before the deadline to do so.

In this article, we'll look at what Circle does, the company's share of the market, financials, and what IPO investors might be able to expect in the early trading of CRCL shares.

CGDV: Forget The Yield, This Active Dividend Value ETF Just Works

The Sunday Investor| Buy

I last reviewed the Capital Group Dividend Value ETF (NYSEARCA:CGDV) on December 11, 2024, when I reiterated my original "buy" rating from September 21, 2023, based on a detailed fundamental analysis and my understanding of the Capital Group's unique multi-manager approach to portfolio construction. As shown, it's outperformed a collection of popular low-yielding dividend funds since that initial review date, and over the last six months, it's pulled ahead of the SPDR S&P 500 ETF (SPY). Certainly, CGDV is building an excellent track record, and its assets under management have now reached an impressive $16.68 billion.

The purpose of this article is to update readers on CGDV's latest fundamentals. Since my last review, there were 11 substitutions to the 52-stock portfolio, and after accounting for allocation changes for the remaining stocks, the two versions of CGDV are roughly 73% similar. That's enough to warrant a deeper dive, but after my closer look at CGDV and some of the competitors above, I remain impressed by its strong combination of quality, growth, and value. Therefore, I'm confident enough to extend my "buy" rating on CGDV.

Conagra Brands: Defensive Dividend Stock Getting To My Buying Target

IWA Research| Hold

Following demand issues that led to falling revenues and profits and now tariffs that could cause more supply chain issues, Conagra Brands (NYSE:CAG) is now down over 40% from its peak. However, I believe we're still looking at a healthy company that's only dealing with some temporary demand issues caused by the current economic conditions, while long-term tailwinds support their product offering. Naturally, this brought them to what, I think, could be a very compelling valuation, with a more than 6% sustainable dividend and an equity almost equal to the market cap.

When QYLD Makes Sense And When It Doesn't

The Alpha Analyst| Buy

My earlier coverage of option writing-based income ETFs on the NDX has seen the mixed performance. While I found JEPQ a nonperformer in correcting or consolidating markets, where it should ideally win, QQQI was relatively better off. In this analysis, I will look at the Global X NASDAQ 100 Covered Call ETF (NASDAQ:QYLD) to see whether it qualifies as a good asset to hold against the alternative route of holding the underlying QQQ (tracking NDX).

For the record, the benchmark for QYLD is the CBOE NASDAQ-100 BuyWrite Index (BXNT- an index that owns the NDX, writes call options at the money every month, and holds till expiration). However, for all practical purposes, I will compare the total returns of QYLD against the core underlying i.e. NDX as tracking error vs the benchmark BXNT is a theoretical exercise. I am interested in total returns, which most investors are. A soundtracking efficiency at the cost of total returns is not a useful investment.

Also note that while the ETF is a rule-based ETF, writing at or near the money call options on NDX, there is scope for active decision-making on how near or which near the money option is chosen. That is critical decisions impacting performance. So, while the rule is passive in nature, its implementation does keep active decisions of the management open.

A-Mark Precious Metals: Follow The Cash Flow

AV Investments| Strong Buy

Throughout my investing career, almost all of my successful investments have had 3 to 4 shared characteristics:

  1. Net cash position (Cash and ST investments > Total Debt)
  2. Returning capital to shareholders, preferably share buybacks
  3. Growing revenue and/or increasing margins
  4. Priced at <15x Free Cash Flow to Enterprise Value

I believe I have found another great investment in A-Mark Precious Metals (AMRK). In my accounting career, I worked for three different precious metals retailers/wholesalers over the span of ~7 years. These companies were privately owned and two of these companies had revenue of around ~$1b per year. With my experience and knowledge of the precious metals industry, I believe A-Mark is undervalued at $21.00/share.

Beta FinTech Holdings Limited (BTFT) has filed to raise $10 million in an IPO of its ordinary shares, according to SEC F-1 registration information.

BTFT provides a range of financial and related services in Hong Kong.

Given the numerous risks the company poses, its tiny size, thin capitalization, high valuation expectations and worsening net results, my outlook on the BTFT IPO is to sell (avoid it).

What Does Beta FinTech Do?

Hong Kong, China-based Beta FinTech Holdings Limited was founded to develop various service offerings to companies operating in the PRC.

Management is led by Chief Executive Officer Mr. Xianxin Xiang, who has been with the firm since 2024 and was previously CEO of FTFT International Securities and Futures Limited.

The company's primary offerings include:

  • Brokerage services

  • Placing

  • Underwriting

  • Financial advisory

  • Margin loans

  • Other.

In the most recent six-month period, the company has generated revenue contribution per this pie chart:

$1.3 million, which is expected to be approximately 20% of the net proceeds, for the applications for brokerage licenses to be made to the relevant regulators in the United States and the following Southeast Asian countries: Malaysia, Thailand, and Vietnam. Beta FinTech Is A Small Company With Worsening Financial Results

BTFT is seeking U.S. public capital market funding for its growth plans within Hong Kong and outside the region.

The firm’s financials have produced growing topline revenue from a tiny base, fluctuating operating profit, falling operating margin but increasing cash flow from operations.

Free cash flow for the twelve months ended December 31, 2024, was negative ($2.3 million).

Selling and Marketing expenses as a percentage of total revenue have grown as revenue has increased from a small base and its Selling and Marketing efficiency multiple fell to 11.9x in the most recent reporting period.

The firm currently plans to pay no dividends and will likely retain any future earnings and cash flows to reinvest back into its growth and working capital needs.

BTFT’s recent capital spending history shows it has continued to spend on capital expenditures despite generating negative operating cash flow.

The market opportunity for providing financial services in Hong Kong is large and will likely grow, but the market features intense competition from companies of all sizes.

Risks to the company’s outlook as a public firm include its "foreign private issuer" and "emerging growth company" status, which enable management to reduce the amount of information it provides to shareholders.

Also, the firm is subject to unpredictable and arbitrary regulatory risks operating in Hong Kong.

BTFT also has significant customer concentration risks, with three clients accounting for 63% of its revenue for the six months ended December 31, 2024.

Beta also has numerous related party transactions, as detailed in the prospectus here.

Beta FinTech is seeking a Price/Sales multiple that is far higher than the U.S. public financial services company benchmark, although its EV/EBITDA multiple is somewhat lower.

Given the numerous risks the company poses, its tiny size, thin capitalization, high valuation expectations and worsening net results, my outlook on the BTFT IPO is to sell (avoid it).

Expected IPO Pricing Date: To be announced.

data.title: Federal Reserve Watch: Money Is Available
data.description:
The times are starting to catch up.

I have been writing over the past year or so about the fact that the Federal Reserve pumped billions of dollars...even trillions...into the banking system to protect the U.S. economy from the COVID-19 disaster.

A lot of this money was not spent as the velocity of circulation of the money stock took a major dip.

Here take a look at what occurred back then.

During the year 2020, the M2 money stock expanded by around 24 percent.

The use of that money, represented by the velocity of circulation of the money stock, dropped off precipitously.

Consequently, the United States did not experience the amount of inflation that would usually accompany this amount of increase in the money stock.

As things settled down in the economy, the M2 money stock tapered off in 2021 and 2022.

The velocity of circulation of the M2 money stock began to rise.

Currently, we see that the M2 money stock has been rising again, and it has now grown to where it exceeds the level it reached in the Spring of 2022.

Analysts have been pointing to this turnaround and have called attention to how this expansion might lead to growing inflation in the United States.

I think that there is greater concern that needs to be expressed just by looking at the figures from January 2022 to the present. I think that we need to go back to January 2000 and really worry about all the money that the Federal Reserve has injected into the banking system since the fight against COVID-19 really started.

Here is the time period I think we should be worried about.

As far as the "use" of money is concerned, the velocity of circulation of the M2 money has returned to about where it was before the beginning of the battle against COVID-19 affects.

But, the velocity of circulation could go higher.

Let's look at what M2 velocity was doing before 2020.

The M2 velocity of circulation had been declining...steadily...before 2020.

So, it is highly possible for the M2 velocity of circulation to move to higher levels in the near future if inflation begins picking up...a real possibility.

This is the environment the Federal Reserve faces. The Federal Reserve must be extremely worried not only about what has happened since early in 2024 when the M2 money stock began to rise once again, but also extremely worried about what happened beginning in early 2020.

For three and one-half years, the Federal Reserve has been reducing the size of its securities portfolio to bring down the number of reserves that exist within the banking system and control the size of the money stock.

It has done a terrific job...so far.

But, there are still a lot of excess reserves in the commercial banking system, almost $3.4 trillion.

These "excess" reserves could be used. The M2 money stock could grow faster.

President Trump could get his wish of faster money stock growth and lower interest rates.

The major point here is that the Federal Reserve would not have to pump a lot more reserves into the commercial banking system.

THE EXCESS RESERVES ARE ALREADY THERE!!!!!

The position of Fed Chairman Jerome Powell and the Fed's FOMC is that the Fed has more work to do.

Excess reserves must be reduced further.

The commercial banking system still has "too much" cash in its vaults.

The fact that the M2 money stock is growing faster now and that the velocity of circulation is rising is an indication that the "excess reserves" of the commercial banking system are being put to work.

Trump does not have to have the Federal Reserve "pump" more reserves into the commercial banking system to get the liquidity for the economic expansion he wants to create.

The reserves are already in the banking system, they just need to be "drawn upon."

There is some indication in the system that people are now drawing on them faster than they were, say, six months ago.

And, this is what Mr. Powell and the Federal Reserve are worried about.

How can we tell that the "cash" is being used?

If the M2 money stock continues to rise and if the M2 velocity of circulation continues to rise...then we have a pretty good idea that the "cash" is being used.

However, this will also be a sign to me that we are moving in the wrong direction.

To me, the Federal Reserve needs to continue to reduce the size of its securities portfolio. That it is, the "excess reserves" in the commercial banking system still need to be reduced, and the amount it needs to be reduced is still a pretty big number.

As far as the actual practice going on.....

In the banking week ending June 4, 2025, the Fed's portfolio of securities held outright dropped by $1.6 billion.

Since the current round of quantitative tightening began, September 18, 2024, the Fed has overseen a reduction in its securities portfolio of $315.5 billion. In addition, loans by the Fed to the commercial banking system have fallen by $94.0 billion.

Since March 16, 2022, when the program of quantitative easing began, the securities portfolio of the Federal Reserve has been reduced by $2.1 trillion.

Reserve Balances with Federal Reserve banks, on June 4, 2025, a "proxy" for the excess reserves in the commercial banking system, totaled $3.4 trillion.

The Federal Reserve, in my mind, still has a long journey to travel until it returns to a more "normal" level of "cash" in the banking system. Therefore, from my viewpoint, the banking system still remains the source of a possible surge in future rates of inflation.

Trump does not need a new Fed chairman to "open the spigots." The money is already there.

data.title: Eaton: A Dip In Stock Price Would Serve Investors Well
data.description:
Company Description

Eaton Corporation plc (NYSE:ETN) is a worldwide power management company with activity in the electrical, aerospace, and vehicle sectors. The company operates through five segments, Electrical Americas, Electrical Global, Aerospace, Vehicle, and eMobility. ETN sports a current market cap of nearly $130B and employs close to 100,000 people. The stock has performed very well over the past decade, achieving a total return of almost 500%, or a CAGR of 19.5%.

Quality Financial Metrics

Let's assess some important quantitative financial metrics for ETN to determine how the company has performed.

Eaton's revenue per share has grown at a clip of 3.8% over the past ten years. For the most part, this metric grew steadily between FY15 and FY19, before a 13% decline in FY20. Following that double-digit decrease, this metric rebounded, with increases in the high-single digit to low-double digit range since. The company has a share buyback program and typically reduces its share count by about 1% per year, with some years better than others.

The gross profit margin oscillated between 30.5% and 38.2% over the past decade, with a total increase of more than 20% during that timeframe. There was an unsurprising decline in FY20, but this metric has since risen steadily, including peaking at 38.2% in the most recent fiscal year.

The return on invested capital for ETN has followed a similar path as the gross profit margin. This metric was generally on an upward trend for the first half of the chart before dropping to 5% in 2020. Following the bottom-out in FY20 the ROIC has bounced back with increases every year since.

Dividend Data

Eaton pays a quarterly dividend of $1.04 per share or $4.16 annually which equates to a current dividend yield of 1.28%. ETN offers some inflation-beating dividend growth, with its 3-year dividend growth rate slightly higher than 8%, while its 5 & 10-year dividend growth rates are north of 6.5%. Aside from 2020, the payout ratio has averaged about 50% over the past ten years and has been declining recently. It's definitely an encouraging sign for dividend investors like myself to see the payout ratio dropping which will hopefully lead to healthy dividend increases in the future.

Q1 2025 Earnings

In early May, Eaton announced their Q1 2025 earnings with a double beat, Non-GAAP EPS was $2.72, topping expectations by $0.01 on revenue of $6.4B which also exceeded the consensus by $150M and was a 7.3% increase year-over-year. Additionally, GAAP EPS was $2.45 surpassing analyst estimates by $0.02.

Looking at the segments individually, we'll start with the Electrical Americas segment which saw revenue surge by 12% to $3B for the quarter, with organic sales up 13%, partially offset by exchange rate headwinds. Furthermore, operating profit for this segment was $904M, a 15% increase from Q1 2024.

The Electrical Global segment of the business had sales of $1.6B, a 7% increase from Q1 2024. This segment faced similar currency headwinds but also had organic sales rise 9%.

Moving on to the Aerospace segment, sales were up 12% to nearly $1B. Additionally, organic sales climbed 13%, while operating earnings followed suit rising 12% for the quarter to a record $226M.

The Vehicle portion of the business struggled mightily with revenue dropping 15% YoY to $617M. The operating margin for the quarter was 15.5%, while the operating profit was a tick below $100M.

Last but not least is the eMobility segment which had a modest 2% increase in sales to $162M. However, this segment earned an operating loss for the quarter due to costs related to new initiatives which management expects to see benefits from in the coming quarters.

Management also reaffirmed its FY25 outlook where they project organic growth in the mid-to-high-single-digits; as well as GAAP EPS growth of 10% at the midpoint ($10.49), with adjusted EPS around $12.00 per share or an increase of 11% YoY.

The stock was rather mute immediately following the earnings report; however, since then, it has risen nearly 11%. The full earnings call can be read/listened to here.

Valuation

The final piece of this puzzle is to determine if ETN is trading for an attractive valuation, and to do so we're going to look at it from a free cash flow perspective. Additionally, to give a little more context on how to view the chart below, the estimated fair value is calculated by dividing the FCF/share for that year by the trailing 5-year average of the FCF yield. The discount line is 10% below the estimated FV, while the premium is 10% above, and finally, the Trim line is 40% above the approximate FV. One final note, you'll notice the chart goes into 2025, and because no complete 2025 Free Cash Flow per share data is available, the trailing twelve-month free cash flow per share is used.

My custom valuation model suggests ETN is trading for a premium at the moment, with a fair price closer to $243. Near the beginning of the chart, the stock traded for a modest discount, before trading for a significant premium in the years following the pandemic. The stock has continued to trade for a premium since 2020, rarely dipping below the trim level.

The projected long-term rate of return for Eaton comes in at almost 7%, with the components of that estimate as follows; a dividend yield of 1.28%, a return to fair value factor of -6.03%, and an expected earnings growth rate of 11.70%. I usually aim for a forward rate of return closer to 10%, so this is somewhat discouraging, although the overvaluation is essentially the root cause.

Final Thoughts

ETN stock has performed well compared to the broad market over the past decade, with multiple years earning a total return exceeding 40%. The company has some decent financial metrics that all appear to be trending higher. The dividend yield isn't anything amazing, but the dividend growth is encouraging, and typically better than inflation. Moreover, aside from the Vehicle segment, all other segments of the business appear to be growing with no signs of slowing down. The one drawback I see here is the valuation, with the stock consistently trading for a premium, investors may be hard-pressed to find a good entry point. Thus, I rate the stock a HOLD, I wouldn't be initiating a position at these levels, but if you bought in at a lower price point I certainly wouldn't be exiting my position anytime soon.

data.title: Salesforce: A Beta Play Delivering Excess Value
data.description:
Salesforce (CRM) is frequently mentioned as a cheap or undervalued stock on account of its depressed pricing multiples. During my analysis I outline why this is not the case, and why the stock is trading at around a two-year premium. Despite this, I view Salesforce entering the stage of moving to increased profitability, and thereby increasing the value for investors. While I see limitations in growth, including M&A driven-growth, I view that the main value factor for the company will be the increase in GAAP operating margins in the future, which I mark as the KPI for investors following the company. Competitors are a main risk factor for the company as the tech becomes easier to replicate. However, the main moat for Salesforce is their established enterprise-level customer base, which is sticky on account of the difficulty in replacing the large system that is Salesforce. The company has higher up-front costs for customer acquisition but can minimize the maintenance costs for retention, which will be the key driver that allows for increased margins over time.

I value Salesforce around $200B, or $204 per share, with a 1-year target price of $230 per share with a positive outlook as I expect the company to become more valuable as it returns 13% p.a. to investors, in excess of my estimated required return of 8.5%.

While stocks are forward-looking, popular names tend to be tied to the near-ish term, i.e. to projections 2-3 years out. This is especially true for stocks that have a stabilized TAM, such as Salesforce or Google. In situations like this, the question is how much will the TAM grow, as opposed to how large it is. In contrast, companies like Palantir and Cloudflare are being speculated on at the TAM-level, where investors try to estimate just how large the target industry is.

Because of this, I view that analyzing Salesforce comes down to the growth of digitalization of the economy, as well as how much can Salesforce charge their customers [enterprise managers] for the privilege of making the “safe choice” when it comes to buying customer relationship management [CRM] software.

Setting Growth Expectations For Salesforce

For Salesforce, Q1 was the first time [since 2019] that the company saw a QoQ revenue top by posting $9.83B, compared to the almost $10B in the previous quarter. Upper revenue guidance for FY '26 was around $41.3B, implying an 8.7% growth.

Growth has been slowly trending down as the company increased its size, and while this is expected, it seems that the AI growth story isn’t enough to offset the lack of organic software growth opportunities.

The chart below illustrates the growth curve for the company on a trailing 12-month basis.

As growth decelerates, the question becomes “can Salesforce revitalize with AI, or will it need another avenue?”

While the AI growth story is compelling, to me, it looks more like a defensive CapEx spend driven by broad AI adoption across the field. It’s not so much that CRM offers a unique customer advantage with AI, but more that they want to eliminate any reasons for existing customers to switch to competitors because of AI. That being said, CRM is as good of an AI use case as it gets. The field benefits from insights on customers, as well as increasing the speed of the sales pipelines across teams.

At the very least, modeling-in growth rates becomes easier as the ceiling keeps closing. This leads me to my growth estimates for Salesforce up to 10% for the next five years, converging on the risk-free thereafter.

Salesforce Will Engage In Disciplined Acquisitions - But It’s Because Of A Depressed M&A Market

Salesforce recently entered into an $8B equity agreement to acquire Informatica (INFA) – A company that produces software allowing customers to migrate and integrate data from multiple vendors to their preferred platform. Salesforce is betting that Informatica’s data management platform will help structure customer data more effectively - driving better performance from its AI models.

While I view Salesforce’s acquisition approach as a mixed bag, I am ok with buying companies in this environment as rough financial conditions allow the company to pay more reasonable prices. In fact, INFA peaked twice just below $40 per share in the last 5 years, and Salesforce managed to get it at a slight premium of $25 compared to the pre-announcement price around $19 per share.

I don’t necessarily mind more acquisitions per se, but the problem I have is that I’m not seeing viable organic growth opportunities for the company, and competitors are chirping away at the software’s moat.

Growing Along Industry Lines

Part of the reason why I estimate that Salesforce will be able to sustain growth rates around 10% is the support from the general industry growth. I estimate the customer relationship market TAM (1, 2) between $76B and $101B, with a 2024 midpoint at $89B. From this, CRM holds some 43%, and rising peers like HubSpot (HUBS) have about 3.4%.

By applying a discount to the estimates from my sources above, I project that the industry will grow to $210B in 2032, a CAGR of 13%. However, I will keep my growth estimates for Salesforce up to 10% as I expect that most of the projected TAM growth will be allocated to companies like HUBS and MNDY that are targeting the SMB [small to medium business] market. In other words, I expect growth to come from smaller businesses adopting CRM solutions but expect them to look for bargains in software pricing.

Given that Salesforce primarily sells its software to larger corporations, and already has a substantial share of this market, I expect it to become more difficult for them to add customers.

The New CRM Landscape

Salesforce’s competitive landscape has changed from being the only game in town for enterprise-level CRM, to facing competitors from the SMB space as well as large peers seeking to expand software across their customers’ sales cycle.

Considering large peers, Salesforce is facing Microsoft with its Dynamics platform as well as Oracle with their CX and ERP offerings. From below, the company primarily faces peers like HUBS that is developing an SMB CRM + Marketing automation platform, as well as monday.com (MNDY) which has recently entered the CRM space.

In my view, most of these companies started off their value proposition within a specialized niche, however, their product offerings are starting to quickly overlap as they attempt to capture an increasing portion of the sales/business cycle. For example, ORCL built a business around relational databases, and expanded to produce ready-made products built around their database core. Microsoft started their journey on the productivity side with Office, and is now expanding by connecting Office to KPIs via Dynamics. HubSpot tailored their CRM to include SMBs’ pain-points such as marketing automation and is now moving up the stack by targeting enterprise-level customers. MNDY started with their “Work OS” [project management & insights], and has now added a customer service and CRM offering to their stack.

Regardless of whether investors think which of these approaches will capture a larger portion of the market, the base case now is that the landscape is more crowded, which I expect to result in stunted margin growth.

From Salesforce, I expect it to continue being the leading enterprise-level CRM due to its brand name and embeddedness in the industry. While there is disruption happening in the space, I view that it will impact Salesforce’s pricing power - as peers increase supplementary products, but won’t lead to switching in large customers.

How Salesforce Stacks-up To Peers

Salesforce has the lowest forward EV/Sales among peers, but it doesn’t mean it is undervalued. In my view, the company also has the lowest growth prospects, so one may go so far as to say the ratio may further compress.

The table below shows how the companies compare both on the fundamentals and pricing:
